data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "Harness strictly beats self-consistency only when the critic's per-candidate error detection is approximately conditionally independent of the majority outcome given candidate correctness AND the critic operates in the high-leverage regime where P(exactly m correct) is maximized; at low k (k=5) this regime is too narrow to reliably hit, but at k >= 11 the binomial mass on the fragility boundary broadens, making strict gains achievable with even a modestly calibrated critic (precision 0.5-0.7, recall 0.5-0.7).", "mechanism": "The probability of a fragile majority (exactly m correct out of k) follows Binomial(k, 1-e) at point m. For k=5, m=3, this peaks at e close to 0.4 but the peak is narrow and the absolute probability is moderate (~0.35). For k=11, m=6, the peak is wider and the absolute probability near e=0.45 is higher (~0.22 but the adjacent cells m=5 and m=7 are also substantial, giving a wider effective fragility zone). The key mechanism is that larger k smooths the fragility window: there are more marginal-majority configurations, so the critic has more opportunities to flip outcomes. However, conditional independence of critic errors from majority outcomes is critical: if the critic is systematically more likely to flag candidates in already-correct majorities (e.g., because confident correct answers look 'suspicious'), false flags cluster on fragile-correct pools and net harm results. The critic must be calibrated such that P(flag | wrong, pool majority) ~= P(flag | wrong, pool minority) and P(no-flag | correct, pool majority) ~= P(no-flag | correct, pool minority).", "scope": {"covers": ["k >= 11 (large jury)", "base error e in broadened fragility window (1/(k+1), k/(k+2))", "critic with approximately conditionally-independent error detection", "modest critic calibration (p in [0.5, 0.7], r in [0.5, 0.7])"]}, "excludes": ["k <= 5: fragility window too narrow for reliable strict gains with modest calibration", "critic whose errors are conditionally dependent on majority outcome (e.g., systematically biased toward flagging in correct majorities)"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "k=11, e=0.42, critic p=0.55, r=0.55 with verified conditional independence: harness score strictly > self-consistency by >= 2 percentage points", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}, {"case": "k=11, e=0.42, critic p=0.55, r=0.55 but critic flags are conditionally dependent on majority (P(flag|correct, fragile-correct) > 2 * P(flag|correct, fragile-wrong)): harness score < self-consistency score", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}, {"case": "k=5, e=0.40, critic p=0.60, r=0.60: harness score == self-consistency score (insufficient fragility mass at small k for modest calibration)", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}, {"case": "k=11, e=0.05, critic p=0.70, r=0.70: e below fragility window, harness score == self-consistency score", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}], "prose_notes": "This is the most atypical candidate because it challenges the neighborhood's focus on k=5 and argues that the observed failures are partly a small-k artifact. The decisive experiment sweeps k across {5, 7, 11, 15} at a fixed e near the fragility peak (e ~ 0.4 for odd k) with a single modestly-calibrated critic, measuring conditional independence of critic flags via a chi-square test on flag-vs-majority contingency. Budget constraint: k=11 requires 11 candidates/question; at 80 questions x 11 x 200 tokens = 176k generation + 80 x 11 x 150 critic = 132k = 308k. Trim to 75 questions or reduce k sweep to {5, 7, 11} to fit 300k. The conditional independence test is the novel discriminative element: it predicts that even a high-precision critic fails if its errors cluster on correct majorities."}
